element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Button alternation mechanic foundation; no timing complexity
Introduces timing gate for jump; angle optimization (42° optimal)
Combines running + action button + angle timing (43° optimal)
Rhythm-based timing: must alternate buttons while jumping on beat
Single-press spin initiation; precise angle timing (45° optimal); discrete input window
Computer-controlled speed; air control via rapid button pressing; angle hold-based launch

typical_tension_triggers: Loss of life on failed heat
Escalating difficulty rounds (higher qualifying levels each cycle)
Precise timing requirements increase per event
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Escalating difficulty structure: early events teach core mechanics with forgiving windows; mid-events layer timing precision; final events demand complex multi-input coordination under strict time constraints. Completion triggers harder round with raised qualifying bars.

difficulty_curve.early_game: 100m dash introduces button-mashing without timing complexity; establishes baseline skill gate
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Long jump and javelin layer angle optimization onto running; hurdles require rhythmic jumping integration; precision windows tighten
difficulty_curve.late_game: Hammer throw demands discrete spin-initiation timing; high jump combines computer-paced movement with player air control and button-press height modulation
notable_spikes: Transition to hurdles: first event requiring synchronized action-button timing during running rhythm
Hammer throw: discrete input model (single-press spin) breaks continuous alternation pattern
Post-completion rounds: qualifying times reset higher, compressing acceptable performance windows

notable_level_patterns.description: Events 1–6 form a curriculum: pure speed (dash) → speed + timing (long jump) → speed + timing + angle (javelin) → speed + rhythm + timing (hurdles) → discrete control + angle (hammer) → speed management + air control (high jump). No event repeats; knowledge compounds forward.

Each event enforces a minimum threshold (running: one heat; field: three attempts). Failure costs one life. Exhausting lives ends session. This creates discrete pass/fail moments rather than continuous skill curve.
Completing all six events loops back to event one with raised qualifying times. No new mechanics introduced; existing mechanics are time-compressed, increasing precision demand.

Each event displays one or two horizontal tracks with large scoreboard (world records vs. current attempt) and background audience. Horizontally scrolling areas (5 independent zones in code) create visual continuity but de-emphasize spatial navigation.

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Track & Field NES article)
